problems.title: Sort List
problems.prerequisite: Merge Two Sorted Lists, Find Middle

problems.title: Double a Number Represented as a Linked List
Reverse Linked List
problems.note: Foundation for Reorder List (235), Sort List (236), Reverse Nodes in k-Group (234). Must be mastered before any other LL problem.

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.note: Direct prerequisite for Sort List (236) and Merge K Sorted Lists (111). Teaches pointer rewiring without losing references.

problems.title: Find Middle of Linked List
problems.note: Fast/slow pointer technique. Prerequisite for Sort List (236), Reorder List (235), Convert Sorted List to BST (240), and Palindrome Linked List (272).

problems.title: Linked List Cycle Detection
problems.note: Floyd's two-pointer cycle detection. Also covers finding the cycle entry node. Asked in every on-site across all target companies.

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
problems.note: One-pass solution using two pointers separated by n steps. Tests off-by-one discipline and dummy node usage.

problems.title: Palindrome Linked List
problems.note: Combines three fundamentals in sequence. Capstone problem for Level 1 LL: find mid, reverse second half, compare, restore (optional).

problems.title: Add Two Numbers
problems.note: Digit-by-digit carry propagation. Directly prerequisite for Double a Number Represented as a Linked List (237). Asked at Razorpay financial rounds.

problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.pattern: Deep Copy with Hash Map or Interleaving
problems.note: Two approaches: O(n) space with hashmap, or O(1) space with node interleaving. The O(1) approach is the Palantir bar signal.

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — do NOT build an explicit adjacency list. The grid IS the graph. Each cell is a node; edges are implicit 4-directional neighbors. Building explicit edges adds O(V+E) space and O(E) construction time with zero benefit. BFS/DFS directly on the grid with a visited array or in-place marking is the correct approach.

problems.title: Is Graph Bipartite?
problems.representation_note: ADJACENCY LIST is correct here. Graph density is unknown but typically sparse (E << V²). The density breakpoint is |E|/|V²| > 1/64 (~1.56%) — only above this does a bit-packed adjacency matrix save space. For bipartite checking you only need to iterate neighbors, making O(degree(v)) adjacency list traversal optimal. An adjacency matrix would waste V²/8 bytes for O(1) edge lookup you never use.
problems.representation_decision: Sparse unknown-density graph → Adjacency list default

problems.title: Clone Graph
problems.representation_note: ADJACENCY LIST is the natural choice — the input is already given as an adjacency list. The key decision here is not representation but traversal strategy for deep copy: BFS with a hashmap (node → clone) ensures each node is visited and cloned exactly once. This is the default representation choice for graphs of unknown density.
problems.representation_decision: Unknown density → Adjacency list (input format drives choice)

title: Level 3 requires the integration of multiple paradigms, such as combining graphs with priority queues or trees with dynamic programming.

problems.title: Word Ladder
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — the graph is never constructed. Each word is a node; two words share an edge if they differ by one letter. Building the full adjacency list upfront is O(N²·L) time and space. Instead, generate neighbors on-the-fly during BFS by mutating each character position. This is the canonical example of implicit graph recognition: the problem never says 'graph' but the solution is BFS on a state space.
problems.representation_decision: Implicit string graph → On-the-fly neighbor generation (never build adjacency structure)

problems.title: Shortest Path in a Grid
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — same principle as Number of Islands (id=3). The grid is the adjacency structure. A common failure pattern is constructing an explicit adjacency list for grid problems, adding unnecessary O(V+E) space and O(E) build time. Run BFS/Dijkstra directly on grid coordinates. The 'node' is a (row, col) tuple; 'neighbors' are the 4 or 8 directional cells within bounds.
problems.representation_decision: Grid → Direct coordinate BFS/Dijkstra (no adjacency structure)

problems.title: Min Cost to Connect Points
problems.representation_note: EDGE LIST required here, not adjacency list. Kruskal's MST algorithm needs all edges sorted by weight — the access pattern is 'iterate edges in weight order', not 'iterate neighbors of a node'. An adjacency list forces O(V²) edge extraction before sorting. Use an edge list of (weight, u, v) tuples directly.
problems.representation_decision: Dense complete graph → Edge list for Kruskal's; Adjacency list + min-heap for Prim's
